title: CRM Profile Backfill Coverage: Profile Enrichment Join 001
businessContext: A CRM export must include every customer, even if the optional profile enrichment table has not arrived yet. A teammate used an INNER JOIN and silently removed customers with missing profiles.
problemStatement: Return all customers with their profile city when available. Customers without a profile should still appear with NULL city. The important part is coverage: the output must preserve every base customer row even when enrichment data is missing. This is a common production issue when optional dimension/profile tables arrive late.
studentTask: Write a LEFT JOIN query returning customer_id, customer_name, and city. Your query will be tested with customers that have no profile and with orphan profile rows.
starterCode:
SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name, p.city
FROM customers c
JOIN customer_profiles p
  ON p.customer_id = c.customer_id
ORDER BY c.customer_id;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  p.city
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN customer_profiles p
  ON p.customer_id = c.customer_id
ORDER BY c.customer_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  p.city
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN customer_profiles p
  ON p.customer_id = c.customer_id
ORDER BY c.customer_id;
explanation: The customer table is the required side of the report, so the enrichment must be a LEFT JOIN. INNER JOIN changes coverage by dropping customers without profiles.
hints: The customers table is the required side of the output; start from it.
If a row can be missing in the enrichment table, an INNER JOIN is risky.
The join should keep unmatched customers and show NULL for missing profile fields.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: customer_id, customer_name, city.
Grain: one row per customer.
Business rule: keep customers even when city is missing; city should be NULL when no profile exists.

sqlTestCases.name: Missing and orphan profiles
sqlTestCases.description: Ensures customers without profiles remain and orphan profile rows do not create extra output rows.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name,
  p.city
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN customer_profiles p
  ON p.customer_id = c.customer_id
ORDER BY c.customer_id;

title: Compensation Dashboard Tie Handling: Workforce Ranking 002
businessContext: The people analytics team is auditing compensation dashboards before leadership review. The dashboard must show the top salary bands inside each department and keep tied salaries instead of dropping them.
problemStatement: Return the employees in the top two salary bands per department. If multiple employees share the same salary band, include all of them. The important part is ranking inside each group, not globally. A query that uses a global LIMIT may look correct on tiny data but fails as soon as each department needs its own leaderboard.
studentTask: Write a read-only SQL query using a ranking window function. The output must contain department, employee_name, and salary. Your query will be tested with salary ties and departments that have fewer than two salary bands.
starterCode:
SELECT department, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 2;
solutionCode:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
explanation: A global LIMIT answers a different question. Ranking must happen inside each department, and DENSE_RANK keeps tied salary bands without gaps.
hints: Do not use a global LIMIT; the ranking resets per department.
Use DENSE_RANK if tied salaries should share the same salary band.
Filter the ranked CTE to the top two ranks, then order the final output deterministically.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: department, employee_name, salary.
Grain: one row per employee who belongs to the top two salary bands inside their department.
Business rule: tied salary bands must be included.

sqlTestCases.name: Ties and small departments
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that tied second salary bands are kept and departments with one employee still work.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;

title: Current Inventory Mart Rebuild: Warehouse Grain Reconciliation 005
businessContext: A warehouse availability table is rebuilt from multiple bin-level snapshots. The business wants current available units by warehouse and product, not by bin.
problemStatement: Aggregate bin-level inventory for the latest snapshot date and return positive stock only. The important part is matching the output grain to the report grain. Bin-level or event-level input often needs to be aggregated before it becomes a warehouse mart.
studentTask: Return warehouse_id, product_id, and available_units for snapshot_date 2026-05-31. Your query will be tested with multiple bins, zero stock, and older snapshots.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
explanation: The source grain is bin-level, while the report grain is warehouse-product. Grouping prevents duplicate-looking rows in the mart.
hints: The source table is at bin grain; the output should be warehouse/product grain.
Filter to the required snapshot date before grouping.
Use HAVING to remove products whose summed availability is zero.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: warehouse_id, product_id, available_units.
Grain: one row per warehouse/product on the latest snapshot date.
Business rule: aggregate bin-level rows and remove zero-stock results.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero stock and older snapshots
sqlTestCases.description: Checks aggregation by bin, latest-date filtering, and zero-stock removal.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;

title: CRM Email Duplicate Cleanup: Case-Normalized Duplicate Detection 007
businessContext: A marketing automation sync is charging the company for duplicate contacts. The CRM stores emails with inconsistent casing, so exact string comparison undercounts duplicates.
problemStatement: Find duplicated email addresses after normalizing email casing. Ignore rows where email is NULL. The important part is normalization before grouping. Production duplicates often hide behind casing, whitespace, or NULL values, so the query must count the same key the business system treats as identical.
studentTask: Return email and duplicate_count for emails appearing more than once after LOWER(email). Your query will be tested with case-only duplicates, NULL emails, and all-unique inputs.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  LOWER(email) AS email,
  COUNT(*) AS duplicate_count
FROM crm_contacts
WHERE email IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY LOWER(email)
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY email;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  LOWER(email) AS email,
  COUNT(*) AS duplicate_count
FROM crm_contacts
WHERE email IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY LOWER(email)
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY email;
explanation: Duplicate checks need the same normalization rules used by the downstream system. Grouping on LOWER(email) catches case-only duplicates.
hints: Group by the normalized email value, not the raw email string.
NULL emails should not become a fake duplicate group.
HAVING is used after GROUP BY to keep only duplicate groups.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: email, duplicate_count.
Grain: one row per normalized duplicated email.
Business rule: compare emails after LOWER(email), ignore NULL emails, and return only counts greater than one.

sqlTestCases.name: NULL and case-only duplicates
sqlTestCases.description: Checks case normalization and confirms NULL emails are ignored.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  LOWER(email) AS email,
  COUNT(*) AS duplicate_count
FROM crm_contacts
WHERE email IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY LOWER(email)
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY email;

title: Completed-Order Coverage Gap: First Activity Retention 008
businessContext: Growth wants a list of customers who have not completed an order so the CRM team can send an activation campaign. The orders feed can contain NULL customer IDs from failed guest checkout events.
problemStatement: Find customers who do not have any completed order. The query should be safe even when the orders table contains NULL customer_id values. The important part is NULL-safe exclusion. In production pipelines, failed events and guest checkouts often create NULL foreign keys that can break NOT IN logic.
studentTask: Write a query that returns customer_id and customer_name for customers with no completed orders. Your query will be tested with NULL customer IDs in the fact table and customers with only cancelled orders.
starterCode:
SELECT customer_id, customer_name
FROM customers
WHERE customer_id NOT IN (
  SELECT customer_id
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_status = 'completed'
);
solutionCode:
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name
FROM customers c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
    AND o.order_status = 'completed'
)
ORDER BY c.customer_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name
FROM customers c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
    AND o.order_status = 'completed'
)
ORDER BY c.customer_id;
explanation: NOT EXISTS is safer than NOT IN when a subquery may return NULL. The correlated check only tests matching completed orders for the current customer.
hints: Avoid NOT IN when the subquery may contain NULL values.
The anti-join should only look for completed orders, not all order statuses.
A NOT EXISTS correlated subquery maps well to the business question: no matching completed order for this customer.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: customer_id, customer_name.
Grain: one row per customer with no completed order.
Business rule: customers with cancelled/returned orders only still qualify; NULLs in orders must not break the result.

sqlTestCases.name: Cancelled-only customers and NULL order keys
sqlTestCases.description: Checks customers with only cancelled orders and protects against NULL fact-table keys.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  c.customer_id,
  c.customer_name
FROM customers c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
    AND o.order_status = 'completed'
)
ORDER BY c.customer_id;

title: Operations Temperature Drift Audit: Operations SLA Metric 012
businessContext: Operations is checking whether a cold-storage warehouse had rising temperatures day over day. The alert should compare each day against the previous recorded day.
problemStatement: Return dates where the warehouse temperature is higher than the previous day in the sample feed. The important part is comparing a row with the previous row in time order. A static threshold is not enough when the business asks for day-over-day movement.
studentTask: Use LAG to compare each reading to the previous reading and return reading_date and temperature_c. Your query will be tested with equal readings, decreasing readings, and the first row in the time series.
solutionCode:
WITH reading_with_previous AS (
  SELECT
    reading_date,
    temperature_c,
    LAG(temperature_c) OVER (ORDER BY reading_date) AS previous_temperature_c
  FROM warehouse_temperature
)
SELECT
  reading_date,
  temperature_c
FROM reading_with_previous
WHERE temperature_c > previous_temperature_c
ORDER BY reading_date;
expectedSql:
WITH reading_with_previous AS (
  SELECT
    reading_date,
    temperature_c,
    LAG(temperature_c) OVER (ORDER BY reading_date) AS previous_temperature_c
  FROM warehouse_temperature
)
SELECT
  reading_date,
  temperature_c
FROM reading_with_previous
WHERE temperature_c > previous_temperature_c
ORDER BY reading_date;
explanation: A static threshold does not answer whether temperature rose compared with the previous day. LAG gives the prior row in time order.
hints: Use LAG to bring the previous temperature onto the current row.
The previous row must be defined by reading_date ordering.
The first row has no previous value, so it should not be returned.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: reading_date, temperature_c.
Grain: one row per reading that increased compared with the previous reading date.
Business rule: equal temperatures and the first reading are not increases.

sqlTestCases.description: Checks that only strict increases are returned.

  SELECT
    reading_date,
    temperature_c,
    LAG(temperature_c) OVER (ORDER BY reading_date) AS previous_temperature_c
  FROM warehouse_temperature
)
SELECT
  reading_date,
  temperature_c
FROM reading_with_previous
WHERE temperature_c > previous_temperature_c
ORDER BY reading_date;

title: Same-Day Delivery SLA Metric: Operations SLA Metric 013
businessContext: The logistics dashboard reports same-day delivery percentage. A recent data load mixed delivered and cancelled orders, so the metric needs to be recomputed from the delivery fact table.
problemStatement: Calculate the percentage of delivery records where order_date equals delivered_date. The important part is building the exact SLA numerator and denominator. Counting rows alone is not a rate, and date equality must be checked per delivery.
studentTask: Return one column named same_day_delivery_pct rounded to two decimal places. Your query will be tested with all-same-day, delayed, and mixed delivery records.
starterCode:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS same_day_delivery_pct
FROM deliveries;
explanation: The denominator is all delivery records in scope, and the numerator is only orders delivered on the same date.
hints: The numerator is deliveries where order_date equals delivered_date.
The denominator is all delivery rows in scope.
Multiply by 100.0 before division so the result is a percentage, not integer division.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: same_day_delivery_pct.
Grain: one overall metric row.
Business rule: same-day deliveries divided by all deliveries, rounded to two decimals.

title: First Activity Return Check: First Activity Retention 014
businessContext: The product team wants to know which users returned the day after their first app activity. The events table has multiple events per user per day.
problemStatement: Find users who had at least one event exactly one day after their first recorded event date. The important part is measuring activity relative to each user's first event date. Multiple events per day should not change the user-level retention answer.
studentTask: Return user_id and first_seen_date for users with next-day activity. Your query will be tested with users who have multiple events on day zero and users returning after more than one day.
solutionCode:
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MIN(event_date) AS first_seen_date
  FROM app_events
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  f.user_id,
  f.first_seen_date
FROM first_seen f
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM app_events e
  WHERE e.user_id = f.user_id
    AND e.event_date = DATE(f.first_seen_date, '+1 day')
)
ORDER BY f.user_id;
expectedSql:
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MIN(event_date) AS first_seen_date
  FROM app_events
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  f.user_id,
  f.first_seen_date
FROM first_seen f
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM app_events e
  WHERE e.user_id = f.user_id
    AND e.event_date = DATE(f.first_seen_date, '+1 day')
)
ORDER BY f.user_id;
explanation: Retention is measured from the first activity date, not an arbitrary grouped row. Use a first_seen CTE and test for a next-day event.
hints: First compute one first_seen_date per user.
Then check whether that same user has an event exactly one calendar day later.
Multiple events on the same day should not create duplicate retained users.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: user_id, first_seen_date.
Grain: one row per retained user.
Business rule: a user qualifies only if they return exactly one day after their first event date.

title: Payroll Top-Band Audit: Workforce Ranking 018
businessContext: The people analytics team is auditing compensation dashboards before leadership review. The dashboard must show the top salary bands inside each department and keep tied salaries instead of dropping them.
problemStatement: Return the employees in the top two salary bands per department. If multiple employees share the same salary band, include all of them. The important part is ranking inside each group, not globally. A query that uses a global LIMIT may look correct on tiny data but fails as soon as each department needs its own leaderboard.
studentTask: Write a read-only SQL query using a ranking window function. The output must contain department, employee_name, and salary. Your query will be tested with salary ties and departments that have fewer than two salary bands.
starterCode:
SELECT department, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 2;
solutionCode:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
explanation: A global LIMIT answers a different question. Ranking must happen inside each department, and DENSE_RANK keeps tied salary bands without gaps.
hints: Do not use a global LIMIT; the ranking resets per department.
Use DENSE_RANK if tied salaries should share the same salary band.
Filter the ranked CTE to the top two ranks, then order the final output deterministically.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: department, employee_name, salary.
Grain: one row per employee who belongs to the top two salary bands inside their department.
Business rule: tied salary bands must be included.

title: Warehouse Product Availability: Warehouse Grain Reconciliation 020
businessContext: A warehouse availability table is rebuilt from multiple bin-level snapshots. The business wants current available units by warehouse and product, not by bin.
problemStatement: Aggregate bin-level inventory for the latest snapshot date and return positive stock only. The important part is matching the output grain to the report grain. Bin-level or event-level input often needs to be aggregated before it becomes a warehouse mart.
studentTask: Return warehouse_id, product_id, and available_units for snapshot_date 2026-05-31. Your query will be tested with multiple bins, zero stock, and older snapshots.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
explanation: The source grain is bin-level, while the report grain is warehouse-product. Grouping prevents duplicate-looking rows in the mart.
hints: The source table is at bin grain; the output should be warehouse/product grain.
Filter to the required snapshot date before grouping.
Use HAVING to remove products whose summed availability is zero.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: warehouse_id, product_id, available_units.
Grain: one row per warehouse/product on the latest snapshot date.
Business rule: aggregate bin-level rows and remove zero-stock results.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero stock and older snapshots
sqlTestCases.description: Checks aggregation by bin, latest-date filtering, and zero-stock removal.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:

  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;

title: Marketing Event Metric Fix: Conditional Engagement Metric 023
businessContext: Marketing noticed CTR moved sharply after a tracking release. You need to recompute click-through rate from raw impression and click events.
problemStatement: Calculate click-through rate percentage per ad as clicks divided by impressions. Avoid divide-by-zero failures. The important part is conditional counting. The metric should divide clicks by impressions, not by all events, and should avoid divide-by-zero failures.
studentTask: Return ad_id and click_through_rate_pct rounded to two decimals. Your query will be tested with ads that have clicks, impressions, and zero-impression cases.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
explanation: CTR needs conditional counts for clicks and impressions. NULLIF prevents a division error when an ad has no impressions in the sample.
hints: Use CASE expressions to count clicks and impressions separately.
The denominator is impressions, not total events.
NULLIF protects the metric when an ad has clicks but no impressions in the test data.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: ad_id, click_through_rate_pct.
Grain: one row per ad.
Business rule: CTR = clicks / impressions * 100, rounded to two decimals; zero impressions should not crash the query.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero-impression ad
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that an ad with clicks but no impressions does not crash the query.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;

title: Payroll Top-Band Audit: Workforce Ranking 024
businessContext: The people analytics team is auditing compensation dashboards before leadership review. The dashboard must show the top salary bands inside each department and keep tied salaries instead of dropping them.
problemStatement: Return the employees in the top two salary bands per department. If multiple employees share the same salary band, include all of them. The important part is ranking inside each group, not globally. A query that uses a global LIMIT may look correct on tiny data but fails as soon as each department needs its own leaderboard.
studentTask: Write a read-only SQL query using a ranking window function. The output must contain department, employee_name, and salary. Your query will be tested with salary ties and departments that have fewer than two salary bands.
starterCode:
SELECT department, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 2;
solutionCode:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
explanation: A global LIMIT answers a different question. Ranking must happen inside each department, and DENSE_RANK keeps tied salary bands without gaps.
hints: Do not use a global LIMIT; the ranking resets per department.
Use DENSE_RANK if tied salaries should share the same salary band.
Filter the ranked CTE to the top two ranks, then order the final output deterministically.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: department, employee_name, salary.
Grain: one row per employee who belongs to the top two salary bands inside their department.
Business rule: tied salary bands must be included.

title: Cancelled Order Leakage Check: Completed-Order Revenue 026
businessContext: A daily revenue mart feeds the founder dashboard. Cancelled orders are present in the order tables and must not inflate product revenue.
problemStatement: Calculate completed-order revenue per product and category from order_items joined to products and orders. The important part is filtering to valid revenue before aggregation. Cancelled or non-completed orders can inflate downstream marts if the status filter is missed.
studentTask: Return category, product_name, and revenue for completed orders only. Your query will be tested with cancelled orders and multi-line completed orders.
starterCode:
SELECT p.category, p.product_name, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
explanation: Revenue facts need the correct order-status filter. Joining to orders before aggregating prevents cancelled orders from leaking into the mart.
hints: Join to orders so the status filter is available before revenue aggregation.
Revenue should be quantity times unit price at the item grain.
Cancelled orders should not contribute even if they have order_items rows.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: category, product_name, revenue.
Grain: one row per product/category.
Business rule: revenue includes completed orders only and equals SUM(quantity * unit_price).

sqlTestCases.name: Cancelled order leakage
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that cancelled order_items do not inflate revenue.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;

title: Finance Threshold Reconciliation: Signed Finance Ledger 027
businessContext: Finance wants accounts with unusually high net balance movement this month. Credits increase balance; debits reduce it.
problemStatement: Compute net balance change per account and return only accounts above 10000. The important part is financial sign handling. Credits and debits move balances in opposite directions, so a plain SUM(amount) can produce a dangerously wrong report.
studentTask: Return account_id and net_balance_change. Your query will be tested with accounts above, below, and exactly equal to the threshold.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  account_id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN transaction_type = 'credit' THEN amount ELSE -amount END) AS net_balance_change
FROM account_transactions
GROUP BY account_id
HAVING net_balance_change > 10000
ORDER BY account_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  account_id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN transaction_type = 'credit' THEN amount ELSE -amount END) AS net_balance_change
FROM account_transactions
GROUP BY account_id
HAVING net_balance_change > 10000
ORDER BY account_id;
explanation: Debits and credits must not be summed with the same sign. Use CASE to apply the correct financial direction before HAVING.
hints: Assign debit amounts a negative sign before aggregation.
Use HAVING because the threshold applies after account-level aggregation.
The rule says greater than 10000, so exactly 10000 should not qualify.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: account_id, net_balance_change.
Grain: one row per account above the threshold.
Business rule: credits are positive, debits are negative, and only net balance changes greater than 10000 qualify.

sqlTestCases.name: Threshold boundary
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that exactly 10000 is excluded and debits reduce the net balance.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  account_id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN transaction_type = 'credit' THEN amount ELSE -amount END) AS net_balance_change
FROM account_transactions
GROUP BY account_id
HAVING net_balance_change > 10000
ORDER BY account_id;

title: Regional Enablement Filter: Reference Dimension Filter 029
businessContext: The analytics team maintains a reference table for markets where regional dashboards should be enabled. The enablement rule is based on either population or area.
problemStatement: Return countries that qualify as large markets by population or area. The important part is translating the business rule exactly. When the rule says either threshold qualifies, using AND silently removes valid records.
studentTask: Return country_name, population, and area_sq_km where population is at least 25,000,000 or area is at least 1,000,000. Your query will be tested with rows that qualify by only one threshold.
starterCode:
SELECT country_name, population, area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
  AND area_sq_km >= 1000000;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;
explanation: The business rule uses OR: a market qualifies if either threshold is met.
hints: Read the rule carefully: qualifying by either threshold is enough.
Use OR, not AND, between population and area rules.
Keep the output columns exactly as requested.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: country_name, population, area_sq_km.
Grain: one row per qualifying country.
Business rule: a country qualifies if population OR area crosses the threshold.

sqlTestCases.name: Either-threshold qualification
sqlTestCases.description: Checks rows that qualify by population only and area only.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;

title: Unique Student Rollup Drill: Distinct Enrollment Threshold 030
businessContext: An ed-tech reporting table powers course capacity planning. Duplicate enrollment events can appear after payment retries, so the report should count unique students.
problemStatement: Find classes with at least five distinct enrolled students. The important part is counting unique students, not enrollment events. Retry events or duplicate rows should not inflate class capacity numbers.
studentTask: Return class_name and enrolled_students. Your query will be tested with duplicate enrollment rows and classes just below the threshold.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;
explanation: Capacity reporting should count students, not events. COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) protects the metric from retry duplicates.
hints: Count distinct student_id, not raw enrollment rows.
The threshold is applied after grouping by class.
Duplicate retry rows should not change whether a class qualifies.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: class_name, enrolled_students.
Grain: one row per qualifying class.
Business rule: count distinct students and keep classes with at least five students.

sqlTestCases.name: Duplicate enrollment retries
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that duplicate student rows do not push a class over the threshold.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;

title: Ad CTR Metric Rebuild: Conditional Engagement Metric 031
businessContext: Marketing noticed CTR moved sharply after a tracking release. You need to recompute click-through rate from raw impression and click events.
problemStatement: Calculate click-through rate percentage per ad as clicks divided by impressions. Avoid divide-by-zero failures. The important part is conditional counting. The metric should divide clicks by impressions, not by all events, and should avoid divide-by-zero failures.
studentTask: Return ad_id and click_through_rate_pct rounded to two decimals. Your query will be tested with ads that have clicks, impressions, and zero-impression cases.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
explanation: CTR needs conditional counts for clicks and impressions. NULLIF prevents a division error when an ad has no impressions in the sample.
hints: Use CASE expressions to count clicks and impressions separately.
The denominator is impressions, not total events.
NULLIF protects the metric when an ad has clicks but no impressions in the test data.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: ad_id, click_through_rate_pct.
Grain: one row per ad.
Business rule: CTR = clicks / impressions * 100, rounded to two decimals; zero impressions should not crash the query.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero-impression ad
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that an ad with clicks but no impressions does not crash the query.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;

title: Warehouse Product Availability: Warehouse Grain Reconciliation 032
businessContext: A warehouse availability table is rebuilt from multiple bin-level snapshots. The business wants current available units by warehouse and product, not by bin.
problemStatement: Aggregate bin-level inventory for the latest snapshot date and return positive stock only. The important part is matching the output grain to the report grain. Bin-level or event-level input often needs to be aggregated before it becomes a warehouse mart.
studentTask: Return warehouse_id, product_id, and available_units for snapshot_date 2026-05-31. Your query will be tested with multiple bins, zero stock, and older snapshots.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
explanation: The source grain is bin-level, while the report grain is warehouse-product. Grouping prevents duplicate-looking rows in the mart.
hints: The source table is at bin grain; the output should be warehouse/product grain.
Filter to the required snapshot date before grouping.
Use HAVING to remove products whose summed availability is zero.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: warehouse_id, product_id, available_units.
Grain: one row per warehouse/product on the latest snapshot date.
Business rule: aggregate bin-level rows and remove zero-stock results.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero stock and older snapshots
sqlTestCases.description: Checks aggregation by bin, latest-date filtering, and zero-stock removal.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;

studentTask: Return warehouse_id, product_id, and available_units for snapshot_date 2026-05-31. Your query will be tested with multiple bins, zero stock, and older snapshots.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
explanation: The source grain is bin-level, while the report grain is warehouse-product. Grouping prevents duplicate-looking rows in the mart.
hints: The source table is at bin grain; the output should be warehouse/product grain.
Filter to the required snapshot date before grouping.
Use HAVING to remove products whose summed availability is zero.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: warehouse_id, product_id, available_units.
Grain: one row per warehouse/product on the latest snapshot date.
Business rule: aggregate bin-level rows and remove zero-stock results.

title: OR Rule Reporting Drill: Reference Dimension Filter 040
businessContext: The analytics team maintains a reference table for markets where regional dashboards should be enabled. The enablement rule is based on either population or area.
problemStatement: Return countries that qualify as large markets by population or area. The important part is translating the business rule exactly. When the rule says either threshold qualifies, using AND silently removes valid records.
studentTask: Return country_name, population, and area_sq_km where population is at least 25,000,000 or area is at least 1,000,000. Your query will be tested with rows that qualify by only one threshold.
starterCode:
SELECT country_name, population, area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
  AND area_sq_km >= 1000000;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;
explanation: The business rule uses OR: a market qualifies if either threshold is met.
hints: Read the rule carefully: qualifying by either threshold is enough.
Use OR, not AND, between population and area rules.
Keep the output columns exactly as requested.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: country_name, population, area_sq_km.
Grain: one row per qualifying country.
Business rule: a country qualifies if population OR area crosses the threshold.

sqlTestCases.name: Either-threshold qualification
sqlTestCases.description: Checks rows that qualify by population only and area only.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;

title: HR Leaderboard Window Function: Workforce Ranking 041
businessContext: The people analytics team is auditing compensation dashboards before leadership review. The dashboard must show the top salary bands inside each department and keep tied salaries instead of dropping them.
problemStatement: Return the employees in the top two salary bands per department. If multiple employees share the same salary band, include all of them. The important part is ranking inside each group, not globally. A query that uses a global LIMIT may look correct on tiny data but fails as soon as each department needs its own leaderboard.
studentTask: Write a read-only SQL query using a ranking window function. The output must contain department, employee_name, and salary. Your query will be tested with salary ties and departments that have fewer than two salary bands.
starterCode:
SELECT department, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 2;
solutionCode:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
explanation: A global LIMIT answers a different question. Ranking must happen inside each department, and DENSE_RANK keeps tied salary bands without gaps.
hints: Do not use a global LIMIT; the ranking resets per department.
Use DENSE_RANK if tied salaries should share the same salary band.
Filter the ranked CTE to the top two ranks, then order the final output deterministically.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: department, employee_name, salary.
Grain: one row per employee who belongs to the top two salary bands inside their department.
Business rule: tied salary bands must be included.

sqlTestCases.name: Ties and small departments
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that tied second salary bands are kept and departments with one employee still work.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (

  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;

title: Unique Student Rollup Drill: Distinct Enrollment Threshold 042
businessContext: An ed-tech reporting table powers course capacity planning. Duplicate enrollment events can appear after payment retries, so the report should count unique students.
problemStatement: Find classes with at least five distinct enrolled students. The important part is counting unique students, not enrollment events. Retry events or duplicate rows should not inflate class capacity numbers.
studentTask: Return class_name and enrolled_students. Your query will be tested with duplicate enrollment rows and classes just below the threshold.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;
explanation: Capacity reporting should count students, not events. COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) protects the metric from retry duplicates.
hints: Count distinct student_id, not raw enrollment rows.
The threshold is applied after grouping by class.
Duplicate retry rows should not change whether a class qualifies.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: class_name, enrolled_students.
Grain: one row per qualifying class.
Business rule: count distinct students and keep classes with at least five students.

sqlTestCases.name: Duplicate enrollment retries
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that duplicate student rows do not push a class over the threshold.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;

title: Founder Dashboard Revenue Fix: Completed-Order Revenue 047
businessContext: A daily revenue mart feeds the founder dashboard. Cancelled orders are present in the order tables and must not inflate product revenue.
problemStatement: Calculate completed-order revenue per product and category from order_items joined to products and orders. The important part is filtering to valid revenue before aggregation. Cancelled or non-completed orders can inflate downstream marts if the status filter is missed.
studentTask: Return category, product_name, and revenue for completed orders only. Your query will be tested with cancelled orders and multi-line completed orders.
starterCode:
SELECT p.category, p.product_name, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
explanation: Revenue facts need the correct order-status filter. Joining to orders before aggregating prevents cancelled orders from leaking into the mart.
hints: Join to orders so the status filter is available before revenue aggregation.
Revenue should be quantity times unit price at the item grain.
Cancelled orders should not contribute even if they have order_items rows.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: category, product_name, revenue.
Grain: one row per product/category.
Business rule: revenue includes completed orders only and equals SUM(quantity * unit_price).

sqlTestCases.name: Cancelled order leakage
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that cancelled order_items do not inflate revenue.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;

title: Zero-Impression CTR Guardrail: Conditional Engagement Metric 048
businessContext: Marketing noticed CTR moved sharply after a tracking release. You need to recompute click-through rate from raw impression and click events.
problemStatement: Calculate click-through rate percentage per ad as clicks divided by impressions. Avoid divide-by-zero failures. The important part is conditional counting. The metric should divide clicks by impressions, not by all events, and should avoid divide-by-zero failures.
studentTask: Return ad_id and click_through_rate_pct rounded to two decimals. Your query will be tested with ads that have clicks, impressions, and zero-impression cases.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
explanation: CTR needs conditional counts for clicks and impressions. NULLIF prevents a division error when an ad has no impressions in the sample.
hints: Use CASE expressions to count clicks and impressions separately.
The denominator is impressions, not total events.
NULLIF protects the metric when an ad has clicks but no impressions in the test data.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: ad_id, click_through_rate_pct.
Grain: one row per ad.
Business rule: CTR = clicks / impressions * 100, rounded to two decimals; zero impressions should not crash the query.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero-impression ad
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that an ad with clicks but no impressions does not crash the query.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;

title: Completed-Order Revenue Mart: Completed-Order Revenue 049
businessContext: A daily revenue mart feeds the founder dashboard. Cancelled orders are present in the order tables and must not inflate product revenue.
problemStatement: Calculate completed-order revenue per product and category from order_items joined to products and orders. The important part is filtering to valid revenue before aggregation. Cancelled or non-completed orders can inflate downstream marts if the status filter is missed.
studentTask: Return category, product_name, and revenue for completed orders only. Your query will be tested with cancelled orders and multi-line completed orders.
starterCode:
SELECT p.category, p.product_name, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
explanation: Revenue facts need the correct order-status filter. Joining to orders before aggregating prevents cancelled orders from leaking into the mart.
hints: Join to orders so the status filter is available before revenue aggregation.
Revenue should be quantity times unit price at the item grain.
Cancelled orders should not contribute even if they have order_items rows.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: category, product_name, revenue.
Grain: one row per product/category.
Business rule: revenue includes completed orders only and equals SUM(quantity * unit_price).

JOIN orders o

WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;

title: Manager Org Salary Snapshot: Workforce Ranking 052
businessContext: The people analytics team is auditing compensation dashboards before leadership review. The dashboard must show the top salary bands inside each department and keep tied salaries instead of dropping them.
problemStatement: Return the employees in the top two salary bands per department. If multiple employees share the same salary band, include all of them. The important part is ranking inside each group, not globally. A query that uses a global LIMIT may look correct on tiny data but fails as soon as each department needs its own leaderboard.
studentTask: Write a read-only SQL query using a ranking window function. The output must contain department, employee_name, and salary. Your query will be tested with salary ties and departments that have fewer than two salary bands.
starterCode:
SELECT department, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 2;
solutionCode:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
explanation: A global LIMIT answers a different question. Ranking must happen inside each department, and DENSE_RANK keeps tied salary bands without gaps.
hints: Do not use a global LIMIT; the ranking resets per department.
Use DENSE_RANK if tied salaries should share the same salary band.
Filter the ranked CTE to the top two ranks, then order the final output deterministically.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: department, employee_name, salary.
Grain: one row per employee who belongs to the top two salary bands inside their department.
Business rule: tied salary bands must be included.

sqlTestCases.description: Checks that tied second salary bands are kept and departments with one employee still work.

  FROM employees
)
SELECT

FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;

title: User Day-One Retention Audit: First Activity Retention 058
businessContext: The product team wants to know which users returned the day after their first app activity. The events table has multiple events per user per day.
problemStatement: Find users who had at least one event exactly one day after their first recorded event date. The important part is measuring activity relative to each user's first event date. Multiple events per day should not change the user-level retention answer.
studentTask: Return user_id and first_seen_date for users with next-day activity. Your query will be tested with users who have multiple events on day zero and users returning after more than one day.
solutionCode:
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MIN(event_date) AS first_seen_date
  FROM app_events
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  f.user_id,
  f.first_seen_date
FROM first_seen f
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM app_events e
  WHERE e.user_id = f.user_id
    AND e.event_date = DATE(f.first_seen_date, '+1 day')
)
ORDER BY f.user_id;
expectedSql:
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MIN(event_date) AS first_seen_date
  FROM app_events
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  f.user_id,
  f.first_seen_date
FROM first_seen f
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM app_events e
  WHERE e.user_id = f.user_id
    AND e.event_date = DATE(f.first_seen_date, '+1 day')
)
ORDER BY f.user_id;
explanation: Retention is measured from the first activity date, not an arbitrary grouped row. Use a first_seen CTE and test for a next-day event.
hints: First compute one first_seen_date per user.
Then check whether that same user has an event exactly one calendar day later.
Multiple events on the same day should not create duplicate retained users.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: user_id, first_seen_date.
Grain: one row per retained user.
Business rule: a user qualifies only if they return exactly one day after their first event date.

sqlTestCases.name: Multiple first-day events

    MIN(event_date) AS first_seen_date
  FROM app_events
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  f.user_id,
  f.first_seen_date
FROM first_seen f
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM app_events e
  WHERE e.user_id = f.user_id
    AND e.event_date = DATE(f.first_seen_date, '+1 day')
)
ORDER BY f.user_id;

title: Current Inventory Mart Rebuild: Warehouse Grain Reconciliation 059
businessContext: A warehouse availability table is rebuilt from multiple bin-level snapshots. The business wants current available units by warehouse and product, not by bin.
problemStatement: Aggregate bin-level inventory for the latest snapshot date and return positive stock only. The important part is matching the output grain to the report grain. Bin-level or event-level input often needs to be aggregated before it becomes a warehouse mart.
studentTask: Return warehouse_id, product_id, and available_units for snapshot_date 2026-05-31. Your query will be tested with multiple bins, zero stock, and older snapshots.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;
explanation: The source grain is bin-level, while the report grain is warehouse-product. Grouping prevents duplicate-looking rows in the mart.
hints: The source table is at bin grain; the output should be warehouse/product grain.
Filter to the required snapshot date before grouping.
Use HAVING to remove products whose summed availability is zero.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: warehouse_id, product_id, available_units.
Grain: one row per warehouse/product on the latest snapshot date.
Business rule: aggregate bin-level rows and remove zero-stock results.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero stock and older snapshots
sqlTestCases.description: Checks aggregation by bin, latest-date filtering, and zero-stock removal.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  warehouse_id,
  product_id,
  SUM(quantity_on_hand) AS available_units
FROM inventory_snapshots
WHERE snapshot_date = '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY warehouse_id, product_id
HAVING SUM(quantity_on_hand) > 0
ORDER BY warehouse_id, product_id;

title: Dimension Qualification Extract: Reference Dimension Filter 060
businessContext: The analytics team maintains a reference table for markets where regional dashboards should be enabled. The enablement rule is based on either population or area.
problemStatement: Return countries that qualify as large markets by population or area. The important part is translating the business rule exactly. When the rule says either threshold qualifies, using AND silently removes valid records.
studentTask: Return country_name, population, and area_sq_km where population is at least 25,000,000 or area is at least 1,000,000. Your query will be tested with rows that qualify by only one threshold.
starterCode:
SELECT country_name, population, area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
  AND area_sq_km >= 1000000;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;
explanation: The business rule uses OR: a market qualifies if either threshold is met.
hints: Read the rule carefully: qualifying by either threshold is enough.
Use OR, not AND, between population and area rules.
Keep the output columns exactly as requested.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: country_name, population, area_sq_km.
Grain: one row per qualifying country.
Business rule: a country qualifies if population OR area crosses the threshold.

sqlTestCases.name: Either-threshold qualification
sqlTestCases.description: Checks rows that qualify by population only and area only.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  country_name,
  population,
  area_sq_km
FROM country_metrics
WHERE population >= 25000000
   OR area_sq_km >= 1000000
ORDER BY country_name;

title: Distinct Enrollment Threshold: Distinct Enrollment Threshold 061
businessContext: An ed-tech reporting table powers course capacity planning. Duplicate enrollment events can appear after payment retries, so the report should count unique students.
problemStatement: Find classes with at least five distinct enrolled students. The important part is counting unique students, not enrollment events. Retry events or duplicate rows should not inflate class capacity numbers.
studentTask: Return class_name and enrolled_students. Your query will be tested with duplicate enrollment rows and classes just below the threshold.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  class_name,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;
explanation: Capacity reporting should count students, not events. COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) protects the metric from retry duplicates.
hints: Count distinct student_id, not raw enrollment rows.
The threshold is applied after grouping by class.
Duplicate retry rows should not change whether a class qualifies.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: class_name, enrolled_students.
Grain: one row per qualifying class.
Business rule: count distinct students and keep classes with at least five students.

sqlTestCases.description: Checks that duplicate student rows do not push a class over the threshold.

  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS enrolled_students
FROM class_enrollments
GROUP BY class_name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) >= 5
ORDER BY class_name;

title: Campaign Click-Through Audit: Conditional Engagement Metric 062
businessContext: Marketing noticed CTR moved sharply after a tracking release. You need to recompute click-through rate from raw impression and click events.
problemStatement: Calculate click-through rate percentage per ad as clicks divided by impressions. Avoid divide-by-zero failures. The important part is conditional counting. The metric should divide clicks by impressions, not by all events, and should avoid divide-by-zero failures.
studentTask: Return ad_id and click_through_rate_pct rounded to two decimals. Your query will be tested with ads that have clicks, impressions, and zero-impression cases.
solutionCode:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;
explanation: CTR needs conditional counts for clicks and impressions. NULLIF prevents a division error when an ad has no impressions in the sample.
hints: Use CASE expressions to count clicks and impressions separately.
The denominator is impressions, not total events.
NULLIF protects the metric when an ad has clicks but no impressions in the test data.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: ad_id, click_through_rate_pct.
Grain: one row per ad.
Business rule: CTR = clicks / impressions * 100, rounded to two decimals; zero impressions should not crash the query.

sqlTestCases.name: Zero-impression ad
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that an ad with clicks but no impressions does not crash the query.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  ad_id,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'click' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) /
    NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'impression' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0),
    2
  ) AS click_through_rate_pct
FROM ad_events
GROUP BY ad_id
ORDER BY ad_id;

title: Delivery Date Quality Audit: Operations SLA Metric 063
businessContext: The logistics dashboard reports same-day delivery percentage. A recent data load mixed delivered and cancelled orders, so the metric needs to be recomputed from the delivery fact table.
problemStatement: Calculate the percentage of delivery records where order_date equals delivered_date. The important part is building the exact SLA numerator and denominator. Counting rows alone is not a rate, and date equality must be checked per delivery.
studentTask: Return one column named same_day_delivery_pct rounded to two decimal places. Your query will be tested with all-same-day, delayed, and mixed delivery records.
starterCode:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS same_day_delivery_pct
FROM deliveries;
explanation: The denominator is all delivery records in scope, and the numerator is only orders delivered on the same date.
hints: The numerator is deliveries where order_date equals delivered_date.
The denominator is all delivery rows in scope.
Multiply by 100.0 before division so the result is a percentage, not integer division.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: same_day_delivery_pct.
Grain: one overall metric row.
Business rule: same-day deliveries divided by all deliveries, rounded to two decimals.

sqlTestCases.name: Mixed SLA performance
sqlTestCases.description: Checks the rate calculation when some deliveries are same-day and some are delayed.

title: E-commerce Item Revenue Rebuild: Completed-Order Revenue 064
businessContext: A daily revenue mart feeds the founder dashboard. Cancelled orders are present in the order tables and must not inflate product revenue.
problemStatement: Calculate completed-order revenue per product and category from order_items joined to products and orders. The important part is filtering to valid revenue before aggregation. Cancelled or non-completed orders can inflate downstream marts if the status filter is missed.
studentTask: Return category, product_name, and revenue for completed orders only. Your query will be tested with cancelled orders and multi-line completed orders.
starterCode:
SELECT p.category, p.product_name, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name;
solutionCode:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
expectedSql:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;
explanation: Revenue facts need the correct order-status filter. Joining to orders before aggregating prevents cancelled orders from leaking into the mart.
hints: Join to orders so the status filter is available before revenue aggregation.
Revenue should be quantity times unit price at the item grain.
Cancelled orders should not contribute even if they have order_items rows.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: category, product_name, revenue.
Grain: one row per product/category.
Business rule: revenue includes completed orders only and equals SUM(quantity * unit_price).

sqlTestCases.name: Cancelled order leakage
sqlTestCases.description: Checks that cancelled order_items do not inflate revenue.
sqlTestCases.expectedSql:
SELECT
  p.category,
  p.product_name,
  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi
JOIN products p
  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;

title: New User Return Signal: First Activity Retention 066
businessContext: The product team wants to know which users returned the day after their first app activity. The events table has multiple events per user per day.
problemStatement: Find users who had at least one event exactly one day after their first recorded event date. The important part is measuring activity relative to each user's first event date. Multiple events per day should not change the user-level retention answer.
studentTask: Return user_id and first_seen_date for users with next-day activity. Your query will be tested with users who have multiple events on day zero and users returning after more than one day.
solutionCode:
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MIN(event_date) AS first_seen_date
  FROM app_events
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  f.user_id,
  f.first_seen_date
FROM first_seen f
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM app_events e
  WHERE e.user_id = f.user_id
    AND e.event_date = DATE(f.first_seen_date, '+1 day')
)
ORDER BY f.user_id;
expectedSql:
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    MIN(event_date) AS first_seen_date
  FROM app_events
  GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT
  f.user_id,
  f.first_seen_date
FROM first_seen f
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM app_events e
  WHERE e.user_id = f.user_id
    AND e.event_date = DATE(f.first_seen_date, '+1 day')
)
ORDER BY f.user_id;
explanation: Retention is measured from the first activity date, not an arbitrary grouped row. Use a first_seen CTE and test for a next-day event.
hints: First compute one first_seen_date per user.
Then check whether that same user has an event exactly one calendar day later.
Multiple events on the same day should not create duplicate retained users.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: user_id, first_seen_date.
Grain: one row per retained user.
Business rule: a user qualifies only if they return exactly one day after their first event date.

  ON p.product_id = oi.product_id
JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;

  SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS revenue
FROM order_items oi

JOIN orders o
  ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
WHERE o.order_status = 'completed'
GROUP BY p.category, p.product_name
ORDER BY p.category, revenue DESC, p.product_name;

title: Same-Day Delivery SLA Metric: Operations SLA Metric 073
businessContext: The logistics dashboard reports same-day delivery percentage. A recent data load mixed delivered and cancelled orders, so the metric needs to be recomputed from the delivery fact table.
problemStatement: Calculate the percentage of delivery records where order_date equals delivered_date. The important part is building the exact SLA numerator and denominator. Counting rows alone is not a rate, and date equality must be checked per delivery.
studentTask: Return one column named same_day_delivery_pct rounded to two decimal places. Your query will be tested with all-same-day, delayed, and mixed delivery records.
starterCode:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS same_day_delivery_pct
FROM deliveries;
explanation: The denominator is all delivery records in scope, and the numerator is only orders delivered on the same date.
hints: The numerator is deliveries where order_date equals delivered_date.
The denominator is all delivery rows in scope.
Multiply by 100.0 before division so the result is a percentage, not integer division.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: same_day_delivery_pct.
Grain: one overall metric row.
Business rule: same-day deliveries divided by all deliveries, rounded to two decimals.

sqlTestCases.name: Mixed SLA performance
sqlTestCases.description: Checks the rate calculation when some deliveries are same-day and some are delayed.

title: Team Pay Ranking Reconciliation: Workforce Ranking 075
businessContext: The people analytics team is auditing compensation dashboards before leadership review. The dashboard must show the top salary bands inside each department and keep tied salaries instead of dropping them.
problemStatement: Return the employees in the top two salary bands per department. If multiple employees share the same salary band, include all of them. The important part is ranking inside each group, not globally. A query that uses a global LIMIT may look correct on tiny data but fails as soon as each department needs its own leaderboard.
studentTask: Write a read-only SQL query using a ranking window function. The output must contain department, employee_name, and salary. Your query will be tested with salary ties and departments that have fewer than two salary bands.
starterCode:
SELECT department, employee_name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 2;
solutionCode:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
expectedSql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    employee_id,
    employee_name,
    department,
    salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (
      PARTITION BY department
      ORDER BY salary DESC
    ) AS salary_band_rank
  FROM employees
)
SELECT
  department,
  employee_name,
  salary
FROM ranked
WHERE salary_band_rank <= 2
ORDER BY department, salary DESC, employee_name;
explanation: A global LIMIT answers a different question. Ranking must happen inside each department, and DENSE_RANK keeps tied salary bands without gaps.
hints: Do not use a global LIMIT; the ranking resets per department.
Use DENSE_RANK if tied salaries should share the same salary band.
Filter the ranked CTE to the top two ranks, then order the final output deterministically.
expectedOutcome:
Columns: department, employee_name, salary.
Grain: one row per employee who belongs to the top two salary bands inside their department.
Business rule: tied salary bands must be included.

sqlTestCases.description: Checks that tied second salary bands are kept and departments with one employee still work.

  FROM employees
)
SELECT
